does: This paper proves that the claimed equivalence between Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is conditional rather than universal, holding only when the RLHF-optimal policy prefers human-preferred responses. The authors demonstrate that when this implicit assumption is violated, DPO optimizes relative advantage over a reference policy rather than absolute human preference alignment, leading to pathological convergence where the policy decreases DPO loss while preferring dispreferred responses. They characterize conditions under which the assumption fails, prove DPO and RLHF optimize fundamentally different objectives in these cases, and introduce Constrained Preference Optimization (CPO) that augments RLHF with constraints to ensure provable alignment with human preferences. The work also provides geometric interpretation through soft margin ranking, revealing fundamental differences in how DPO implements these objectives.
connects: This work directly addresses the theoretical foundations of DPO, which emerged as a simpler alternative to RLHF for aligning language models with human preferences. It builds on the original DPO paper's theoretical claims of equivalence to RLHF while engaging with broader literature on preference-based learning and alignment. The paper connects to prior work on RLHF's scalability challenges and the motivation for developing simpler methods, while also drawing on preference optimization theory and constraint-based learning frameworks. It responds to growing adoption of DPO in practice without full understanding of its limitations, engaging with work on implicit assumptions in machine learning theory and the gap between theoretical guarantees and empirical behavior in alignment methods.
enables: This work enables more rigorous development of preference optimization methods by identifying when and why DPO fails, providing a foundation for improved algorithms with provable guarantees. CPO demonstrates how constraints can ensure alignment, pointing toward a new class of constrained preference optimization methods that trade off simplicity for theoretical guarantees. The geometric interpretation via soft margin ranking opens avenues for analyzing and designing other preference learning methods with clearer margin-based objectives. The characterization of failure modes enables practitioners to diagnose when DPO may produce misaligned policies, supporting safer deployment of preference optimization in language model training. This work also motivates fundamental research into when simpler training methods can maintain theoretical guarantees, and how to verify alignment properties without expensive RL training pipelines.
